prompt: What is the role of need for closure on misinformation acceptance?
article:
### 1) Core concepts

#### Need for cognitive closure (NFC / NCC / NFCC)
*Need for closure* is an **epistemic motivation**: the desire to reach a **definite answer** and avoid **ambiguity/uncertainty**, often summarized as wanting “an answer… any answer… compared to confusion and ambiguity” [[37]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7815301/). In *lay epistemic theory*, NFC matters because judgment and knowledge formation have no natural stopping point; NFC supplies a motive to stop searching and settle on a conclusion [[22]](https://edwardorehek.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/kruglanskiorehekdechesnepierro2010.pdf).

**Key processing dynamics: “seizing” and “freezing.”**  
NFC is theorized to work through two linked tendencies [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[35]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8637961/):
- **Urgency / seizing:** a push to reach closure quickly (terminate uncertainty fast).  
- **Permanence / freezing:** a push to maintain closure once reached (avoid reopening the question).

This produces well-documented cognitive consequences under higher NFC: **less extensive information processing**, heavier reliance on **early cues** (primacy/anchoring), more **stereotypic/prototypical** (vs diagnostic) evidence use, and greater **confidence** in judgments [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf).

**Trait vs state.**  
NFC is treated as both:
- a **stable individual-difference** trait (people differ reliably), and
- a **situationally inducible state**, increased when closure is valuable or uncertainty is costly (e.g., time pressure, noise, fatigue) and decreased by accountability/accuracy goals [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[35]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8637961/).

**Measurement.**
- The classic **Need for Closure Scale (NFCS)** was introduced by Webster & Kruglanski (1994) as a 42-item measure with five facets: **order/structure**, **predictability**, **discomfort with ambiguity**, **decisiveness**, **closed-mindedness** [[37]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7815301/), [[39]](http://www.communicationcache.com/uploads/1/0/8/8/10887248/individual_differences_in_need_for_cognitive_closure.pdf).  
- Psychometrics: reported strong internal consistency for the overall scale and moderate facet reliabilities, plus test–retest stability over weeks [[39]](http://www.communicationcache.com/uploads/1/0/8/8/10887248/individual_differences_in_need_for_cognitive_closure.pdf).  
- **Debates/updates:** Critics argued the NFCS is **multidimensional** and overlaps with related constructs (e.g., personal need for structure), cautioning against treating it as purely unidimensional [[40]](https://asu.elsevierpure.com/en/publications/what-the-need-for-closure-scale-measures-and-what-it-does-not-tow/), [[41]](https://scholar9.com/publication/06c66b797883a1af8c7b7574dab1f6ce.pdf). Another line of critique focused on the **decisiveness facet** tapping *ability* rather than *need*; revised items were proposed and widely adopted [[43]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17259586/), [[44]](https://biblio.ugent.be/publication/1255265/file/6753166.pdf).  
- A validated **15-item short form** (Roets & Van Hiel, 2011) is commonly used when survey length is constrained and shows good structural fit and strong stability [[44]](https://biblio.ugent.be/publication/1255265/file/6753166.pdf).  
- A narrower **urgency/permanence**-oriented measure (RT-NFC) has been described as distinguishing the two tendencies more directly, though some details are often drawn from secondary summaries [[46]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8795760/).

---

#### What counts as “misinformation acceptance” in research?
Across cognitive psychology, political psychology, and communication research, *misinformation acceptance* is operationalized in several (related but distinct) ways:

1) **Belief / perceived accuracy of false claims**  
Participants rate whether headlines or statements are accurate/true; higher accuracy ratings for false items = greater acceptance. This is central in headline paradigms used in political misinformation work [[1]](https://moodle2.units.it/pluginfile.php/585423/mod_resource/content/0/Pennycook%20%20Rand%20%282019%29.pdf), [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6279465/).

2) **Poor truth discernment (discrimination failure)**  
Acceptance can be represented as weaker ability to distinguish true from false content (often “discernment” = rating(true) − rating(false)) [[1]](https://moodle2.units.it/pluginfile.php/585423/mod_resource/content/0/Pennycook%20%20Rand%20%282019%29.pdf), [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10991074/).

3) **Sharing intentions / propagation**  
People may share content they do not fully believe; sharing tasks measure willingness to disseminate misinformation (an important downstream form of “acceptance” in social systems) [[2]](https://ide.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/Pennycook%20et%20al%20-%20Shifting%20attention%20to%20accuracy.pdf), [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8641132/). Work in this tradition highlights that accuracy judgment and sharing intention can diverge strongly [[2]](https://ide.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/Pennycook%20et%20al%20-%20Shifting%20attention%20to%20accuracy.pdf).

4) **Persistence after correction (continued influence)**  
Even after retraction/correction, misinformation can still shape reasoning; acceptance is measured as continued use of misinformation in inference questions and causal attribution judgments [[6]](https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/features/xlm-0000155.pdf), [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26173286/).

5) **Memory incorporation (misinformation effect)**  
In eyewitness-style paradigms, acceptance is incorporation of misleading post-event details into later recall/report [[78]](https://arch.ies.gov.pl/images/PDF/2019/vol_118/118_Hejniak_m.pdf).

6) **Familiarity/repetition-driven belief (illusory truth)**  
Repetition increases perceived truth/accuracy for claims, including fake news headlines; acceptance is the repetition-driven boost in perceived accuracy [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6279465/), [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8821071/).

A crucial implication: NFC can plausibly relate differently to **belief**, **sharing**, and **persistence**—so “NFC increases misinformation acceptance” is not a single effect, but a family of effects depending on outcome definition.

---

### 2) Why NFC should matter for misinformation: main mechanisms

#### Mechanism A: Reduced open-ended processing → greater reliance on heuristics
High NFC is characterized by **shortened hypothesis generation/testing** and a push to settle quickly [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[22]](https://edwardorehek.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/kruglanskiorehekdechesnepierro2010.pdf). In misinformation environments where cues are noisy and verification is costly, this predicts:
- more reliance on **surface cues** (source familiarity, fluency, consensus cues),
- less active search for disconfirming evidence,
- faster “good enough” acceptance of a closure-providing narrative.

This aligns with broader findings that **time pressure** reduces discrimination between true and false headlines (lower “sensitivity” in signal detection terms) [[68]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9794823/). Time pressure is not identical to NFC, but it is one of the classic situational conditions that closure theory treats as increasing the value of quick closure [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf).

#### Mechanism B: “Seizing” on accessible explanations under uncertainty (especially conspiratorial / simplifying accounts)
When events are ambiguous, misinformation often provides **simple causal stories**. High NFC should increase the attractiveness of these stories because they reduce uncertainty [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full). However, *what* people seize on depends on *availability and accessibility* of explanations.

A key empirical boundary condition is that high NFC does not always mean “more conspiracy.” If a clear official explanation is available and salient, high NFC can “freeze” on it instead and show **lower** conspiracy endorsement [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf).

#### Mechanism C: “Freezing” once belief crystallizes → correction resistance and continued influence
Once a person has reached closure, permanence/freezing predicts:
- resistance to belief change,
- selective discounting of disconfirming evidence,
- stronger “stickiness” of first impressions and early explanations [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf).

This maps naturally onto misinformation phenomena like **continued influence** (misinformation persists despite correction) [[6]](https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/features/xlm-0000155.pdf), [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26173286/), although (important limitation) much of the correction literature does not always measure NFC directly. NFC theory nonetheless predicts a stage-like effect: high NFC may increase susceptibility *early* (when people lack a settled view) but increase resistance to correction *later* (after crystallization) [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[48]](http://www.communicationcache.com/uploads/1/0/8/8/10887248/motivated_resistance_and_openess_to_persuasion_in_the_presence_or_absence_of_prior_information.pdf).

#### Mechanism D: Confirmation-biased evaluation of ambiguous evidence
In conspiracy-belief work, NFC is explicitly linked to **motivational heuristics**: higher NFC → more reliance on **confirmation heuristics** that strengthen existing beliefs; lower NFC → more systematic scrutiny [[23]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3694217/). In misinformation contexts, that implies:
- greater acceptance of *congenial* misinformation (fits priors/identity),
- stronger discounting of challenging corrections,
especially when evidence is mixed and ambiguity allows motivated interpretation.

#### Mechanism E: Reduced epistemic vigilance
“Epistemic vigilance” refers to cognitive mechanisms that protect against being misinformed—argument evaluation, plausibility checking against background knowledge, and competence/trustworthiness assessment of sources [[29]](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1331363/), [[30]](https://www.jasss.org/26/3/8.html). High NFC should tend to:
- truncate argument evaluation (“I’ve heard enough”),
- substitute detailed checking with faster cues (authority/ingroup consensus),
- accept closure-providing messages when they “feel right” or come from trusted ingroup sources.

This mechanism connects NFC to both belief and sharing: if vigilance is reduced, people not only believe more readily but may also share without careful verification.

#### Mechanism F: Group-centrism and identity-based closure
NFC has a group-level counterpart: high NFC is theorized to promote group centrism—pressures toward opinion uniformity, autocratic leadership, rejection of deviates, norm maintenance [[28]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16478302/). In polarized media ecosystems, this implies:
- increased acceptance of misinformation that is normatively endorsed by one’s group,
- greater resistance to outgroup fact-checks or corrections framed as dissent.

Relatedly, **uncertainty-identity theory** predicts that uncertainty increases attraction to highly entitative groups and unambiguous, prescriptive belief systems [[27]](https://sites.bu.edu/marshfellows/files/2021/05/0963721414540168.pdf). This complements NFC: when uncertainty is high, closure-motivated individuals may adopt rigid narratives (including misinformation) that provide identity certainty and behavioral guidance.

#### Mechanism G: Fluency/familiarity pathways (illusory truth) may be amplified by closure motivation
Illusory truth work shows repetition increases perceived accuracy for fake news headlines—even when items are tagged as disputed during exposure and even after delays [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6279465/). Separately, a truth-effect study found that **higher dispositional NCC is associated with a larger truth effect** (greater repetition → perceived truth), consistent with stronger reliance on fluency as a heuristic [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8821071/). This provides a plausible cognitive route: closure motivation may increase the weight given to the “feels true / familiar” cue.

---

### 3) What the evidence says (and what it does *not* say)

#### 3.1 Overall pattern: NFC is a **real but typically modest** predictor, and highly context-dependent
Across the accessible evidence base here, NFC shows:
- **small-to-modest** relationships with conspiracy/misinformation belief outcomes when detectable,
- frequent **null results** or results that depend on salience/uncertainty/trust,
- stronger evidence for *boundary conditions* than for a large simple main effect.

A large representative panel study in Germany (GESIS Panel) found that **pre-pandemic NCC predicts later COVID-19 conspiracy belief**, but explicitly emphasizes that the **effect size is small**, while trust in political/medical institutions is much more strongly (negatively) related to conspiracy belief [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full). Notably, the study preregistered moderation hypotheses (trust × NCC) but found **no moderation** by political trust [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full). This is important: it suggests NCC is not easily “switched off” by trust in a simple interaction pattern (at least in that setting), even though trust is a dominant main predictor.

#### 3.2 Strongest direct experimental boundary-condition evidence: uncertainty × accessibility
Marchlewska et al. (2018) provide one of the clearest demonstrations that the NFC→(mis)information relationship depends on whether conspiratorial explanations are accessible and whether the event lacks a clear official account [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf). In their plane-crash paradigm:
- Under uncertainty and conspiracy salience, higher NFCC tends to predict more conspiracy endorsement (in at least one simple-slope test, marginally) [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf).
- Under certainty (clear official explanation) with conspiracy salience, higher NFCC predicts **less** conspiracy endorsement (a reversal) [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf).
They interpret this as “seizing” on whichever explanation most efficiently provides closure in context.

**Implication for misinformation acceptance more broadly:** high NFC is best thought of as increasing demand for *closure-providing explanations*, not intrinsically “pro-misinformation.” If credible closure is provided by reliable institutions and communicated clearly, high NFC could—under some conditions—reduce misinformation acceptance by freezing on the official account.

#### 3.3 Evidence that reducing closure pressure can weaken belief-consistent interpretation
Leman & Cinnirella (2013) reported that experimentally lowering NFCC (via an accountability manipulation) **reduced the influence of prior conspiracy beliefs on interpretations of evidence** for a novel event [[23]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3694217/). This is not a headline-sharing paradigm, but it is direct evidence that closure-related motivation can causally shape *how evidence is integrated*, consistent with the systematic-vs-confirmatory processing account.

#### 3.4 Direct “fake news belief” evidence beyond conspiracies (headline paradigms)
Evidence here is thinner than for conspiracy belief, but there are notable findings:

- In Hungary’s 2019 election context, higher *need for closure* predicted greater belief in **non-political fake news** in at least one multivariable model (B = 0.20, t = 3.42) [[58]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8740309/). However, the authors emphasize **conspiracy mentality** as the only predisposition that consistently explained belief across *all* fake news types [[58]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8740309/), suggesting NFC is not the most robust predictor when many traits are considered simultaneously.

- A large individual-participant-data meta-analysis of headline veracity judgments highlights robust roles of **analytic thinking** and other factors in misinformation susceptibility [[63]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409329121). NFC is not featured in the visible summary, indirectly suggesting that NFC is either less consistently measured across headline experiments or less central than analytic thinking in that particular synthesis [[63]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409329121).

#### 3.5 Misinformation “acceptance” as memory incorporation: eyewitness misinformation effect
A forensic misinformation study found that higher NFCC correlated with greater susceptibility to post-event misinformation (misinformation effect) [[78]](https://arch.ies.gov.pl/images/PDF/2019/vol_118/118_Hejniak_m.pdf). The authors’ rationale matches closure theory: in ambiguity, high NFCC individuals may rely on available external information (even if misleading) as an anchor to resolve uncertainty [[78]](https://arch.ies.gov.pl/images/PDF/2019/vol_118/118_Hejniak_m.pdf).

This matters because it shows NFC is not only about political ideology; it can relate to **basic cognitive vulnerability** to misleading information under uncertainty.

#### 3.6 Repetition/familiarity routes: truth effect moderation by NCC
A truth-effect study reported that **higher NCC is associated with a stronger truth effect** (repetition increases perceived truth more for high-NCC individuals), and that affective manipulations (e.g., giving an alternative attribution for affect) can reduce the truth effect [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8821071/). Combined with work showing repetition increases perceived accuracy of fake news headlines and persists over time [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6279465/), this supports a credible pathway from closure motivation → fluency reliance → increased belief in repeated misinformation.

#### 3.7 Acceptance of *mitigation tools* (not misinformation itself): warning-tag attitudes
Interestingly, NFCC can also predict receptivity to interventions. A study on misinformation warning tags found NFCC was associated with **more positive attitudes toward warning tags** and self-reported behaviors around tagged content (e.g., presumably being more receptive to warnings), controlling for covariates [[88]](https://escholarship.org/content/qt4h12v5s8/qt4h12v5s8.pdf). This is an important nuance: high closure motivation might increase acceptance of *clear authoritative labels* that resolve uncertainty (i.e., “this is misinformation”), depending on trust in the labeling authority.

---

### 4) Key moderators and boundary conditions

#### (1) Uncertainty/threat and crisis conditions
Crises (pandemic, war, rapid changes) elevate uncertainty and increase the appeal of sense-making narratives. COVID-19 work frames conspiracy beliefs as one route to restore clarity and predictability under threat, especially for those who struggle with closure [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full). A Polish study suggests conspiracy beliefs can function as a mediator between fear and closure-related traits (avoidance of ambiguity, closed-mindedness), consistent with an affect-regulation account where conspiracies reduce fear by providing “answers” [[57]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9690611/).

#### (2) Availability and clarity of official explanations
Marchlewska et al. show the NFC→conspiracy link can reverse when a clear official explanation is available [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf). Practically, this means:
- when institutions provide **timely, clear, coherent causal narratives**, high NFC may freeze on them;
- when institutions provide **uncertainty, inconsistency, or silence**, high NFC may seize on alternative narratives that fill the gap (often misinformation).

This aligns with classic correction findings that effective debunking often requires providing an **alternative explanation** (so you don’t leave a causal vacuum), because retractions alone may not remove continued influence [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26173286/).

#### (3) Stage of belief formation: pre-crystallization vs post-crystallization
Classic NFC work on persuasion demonstrates that high NFC can increase persuadability when people lack a prior informational base (seeking closure), but increase resistance when they already have a crystallized view (freezing) [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[48]](http://www.communicationcache.com/uploads/1/0/8/8/10887248/motivated_resistance_and_openess_to_persuasion_in_the_presence_or_absence_of_prior_information.pdf). Translating to misinformation:
- **early** in a breaking-news event, high NFC may predict faster acceptance of the first plausible explanation (true or false);
- **later**, after commitment, high NFC may predict stronger resistance to corrections and higher continued influence.

This stage-based prediction is plausible and theory-grounded, but still not widely tested in modern social-media misinformation paradigms.

#### (4) Time pressure and accelerated environments
Time pressure reduces discrimination between true and false headlines [[68]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9794823/). Because high NFC involves urgency toward closure, accelerated environments should disproportionately encourage NFC-consistent responding (quick seizing). However, most time-pressure misinformation studies do not measure NFC directly, so the moderation claim remains a hypothesis rather than a demonstrated interaction.

#### (5) Institutional trust
Representative panel evidence shows trust in political/medical institutions is strongly negatively associated with COVID conspiracy beliefs, while NCC is a smaller predictor [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full). Trust can also mediate closure-related pathways to behavior and well-being in cross-cultural settings [[67]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9255507/). The simplest synthesis is:
- high NFC increases demand for certainty;
- trust influences *which* certainty-providing sources are acceptable (institutional vs alternative).

Yet at least one preregistered test did not find the expected trust × NCC moderation on conspiracy belief [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full), implying the relationship may be additive or may require different operationalizations (e.g., trust in specific authorities relevant to the claim).

#### (6) Measurement facets matter (ambiguity avoidance vs decisiveness)
Some evidence suggests different closure-related facets can relate differently to irrational beliefs (e.g., ambiguity intolerance vs decisiveness) [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16772859/). This matters for misinformation because:
- “decisiveness” can sometimes reflect confidence/ability rather than motivational avoidance of ambiguity (hence the measurement revisions) [[43]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17259586/), [[44]](https://biblio.ugent.be/publication/1255265/file/6753166.pdf);
- studies using total scores vs facet scores may produce mixed findings.

#### (7) Education and cognitive resources are not uniformly protective when closure motives are high
A 2026 study reports that education’s protective association against conspiracy beliefs and misinformation susceptibility can be offset by traits including (less consistently) need for closure, such that benefits of education disappear at moderately elevated levels of these traits [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886925005306). This supports a key applied point: high-NFC individuals may not benefit as much from knowledge-based interventions alone if motivational closure needs dominate.

---

### 5) How NFC compares to nearby constructs (based on the accessible evidence)

#### Analytic thinking / cognitive reflection
Analytic thinking is repeatedly shown to predict better discernment in headline paradigms (e.g., lower belief in fake headlines and higher real–fake discrimination) [[1]](https://moodle2.units.it/pluginfile.php/585423/mod_resource/content/0/Pennycook%20%20Rand%20%282019%29.pdf). Large-scale synthesis also emphasizes analytic thinking as a robust correlate of discrimination ability [[63]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409329121). In multivariable contexts, closure measures often appear smaller and less consistent than analytic thinking (where both are measured), though direct head-to-head comparisons are not always reported in accessible excerpts.

#### Conspiracy mentality / conspiracist ideation
In at least one election-context fake-news study, conspiracy mentality was the most consistent predictor across fake-news categories [[58]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8740309/). This suggests NFC may act more as a *general processing motive*, while conspiracy mentality reflects a more domain-specific interpretive lens that directly maps onto many misinformation narratives.

#### Need for cognition (enjoyment of effortful thinking)
Need for cognition tends to predict *lower* misperceptions and more effective responses to corrective information in some contexts, but evidence is mixed and task-dependent. For example, in a correction paradigm, fluid intelligence predicted correction while need for cognition did not [[85]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11411052/). This suggests that “liking to think” is not always sufficient for integrating corrections; cognitive capacity/skills can matter more in some tasks.

#### Trust variables
Trust repeatedly appears as a stronger predictor than closure in COVID conspiracy outcomes [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full) and as influential in tag attitudes [[88]](https://escholarship.org/content/qt4h12v5s8/qt4h12v5s8.pdf). This reinforces a practical framing: NFC creates a *need* for certainty; trust determines *who can provide it*.

---

### 6) Practical implications: what NFC implies for mitigation strategies

No intervention literature in the accessible set cleanly reports “this works especially well for high-NFC people” via NFC-by-treatment interactions. But NFC theory plus the strongest demonstrated intervention mechanisms yield several concrete, testable implications.

#### 6.1 “Fill the vacuum”: provide coherent alternative explanations, not just negation
Because closure motivation is partly about avoiding open questions, debunking that only says “false” can leave uncertainty intact. Classic misinformation correction work emphasizes that retractions often fail and continued influence persists; providing an alternative causal account is a central recommendation [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26173286/). For high-NFC audiences, this is especially important: removing a closure-providing falsehood without supplying an alternative can encourage re-seizing on the original misinformation or on a new misleading explanation.

#### 6.2 Reduce speed / increase deliberation at key choice points (sharing and belief)
Interventions that “slow down” are consistent with counteracting urgency/seizing:
- **Accuracy prompts**: nudges that shift attention to accuracy reliably improve sharing discernment in large samples [[2]](https://ide.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/Pennycook%20et%20al%20-%20Shifting%20attention%20to%20accuracy.pdf), and a large COVID-19 experiment shows several brief prompts can increase sharing discernment substantially [[69]](https://misinforeview.hks.harvard.edu/article/developing-an-accuracy-prompt-toolkit-to-reduce-covid-19-misinformation-online/).  
- **Friction**: adding steps or delays to sharing is argued to promote deliberation and reduce misinformation spread in fast environments [[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12583192/).  
- **Explain-why prompts**: requiring users to explain why a headline is true/false reduces willingness to share false headlines [[13]](https://misinforeview.hks.harvard.edu/article/pausing-reduce-false-news/).

Even though these are not tailored to NFC, they operationally oppose NFC’s urgency tendency (seizing), so NFC theory predicts they may be particularly useful where high NFC is prevalent or situationally induced (crises, high time pressure).

#### 6.3 Protect against repetition-driven belief
Repetition increases perceived accuracy of fake news and persists over time [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6279465/). An accuracy mindset at exposure can eliminate exposure-induced belief increases even among professional content moderators [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11574866/). If high NCC amplifies fluency-based truth effects [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8821071/), then “accuracy mindset at first exposure” is a theoretically well-matched countermeasure.

#### 6.4 Prebunking/inoculation as “closure-compatible” training
Inoculation-style prebunking (e.g., the **Bad News** game) reduces perceived reliability of misinformation and increases confidence in judgments [[73]](https://journalofcognition.org/articles/10.5334/joc.91), with cross-cultural evidence and limited moderation by ideology in the cited studies [[72]](https://misinforeview.hks.harvard.edu/article/global-vaccination-badnews/), [[73]](https://journalofcognition.org/articles/10.5334/joc.91). This may be especially relevant for high-NFC individuals because it provides *structured rules* (“these are manipulation techniques”)—a form of cognitive closure that is aligned with accuracy rather than with any specific narrative.

#### 6.5 Use trusted epistemic authorities—but note that trust is uneven
Given the strong role of institutional trust for COVID conspiracy beliefs [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full), and the fact that high NFC may freeze on authoritative consensus when accepted as legitimate, mitigation often needs:
- credible messengers matched to the audience’s trusted authorities,
- consistent messaging to prevent reopening uncertainty and motivating alternative-seeking.

Warning-tag attitudes can be positively associated with NFCC [[88]](https://escholarship.org/content/qt4h12v5s8/qt4h12v5s8.pdf), suggesting that clear labels can satisfy closure needs—**if** the labeling institution is accepted as competent and legitimate.

---

### 7) What remains unresolved / open problems

1) **True meta-analytic magnitude for NFC specifically (not just “epistemic motives”).**  
A very large Psychological Bulletin meta-analysis reports small pooled correlations for broad motive classes (epistemic motives r ≈ .14; existential r ≈ .16; social r ≈ .16) and substantial heterogeneity [[61]](https://research-portal.uu.nl/ws/portalfiles/portal/257106456/Reasons_to_Believe_A_Systematic_Review_and_Meta-Analytic_Synthesis_of_the_Motives_Associated_With_Conspiracy_Beliefs.pdf). However, the specific pooled effect size for “need for structure/closure” was not extractable from the accessible materials here, and there are indications of potential effect-size inflation for structure/closure motives [[62]](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Mikey-Biddlestone/publication/359841963_A_systematic_review_and_meta-analytic_synthesis_of_the_motives_associated_with_conspiracy_beliefs/links/625fc7ec1c096a380d12cb3e/A-systematic-review-and-meta-analytic-synthesis-of-the-motives-associated-with-conspiracy-beliefs.pdf). The field would benefit from clearer standardized reporting of NFC effects across misinformation outcomes.

2) **Causal identification in modern social-media paradigms.**  
Classic NFC work demonstrates causal effects via situational manipulations (time pressure, accountability, noise) [[21]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf), [[48]](http://www.communicationcache.com/uploads/1/0/8/8/10887248/motivated_resistance_and_openess_to_persuasion_in_the_presence_or_absence_of_prior_information.pdf), and conspiracy evidence shows context interactions [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf). But direct experiments that manipulate NFC and then measure *headline belief/sharing* at scale remain limited in the accessible set.

3) **Outcome separation: belief vs sharing vs persistence.**  
Misinformation acceptance is multi-component. People often share without attending to accuracy [[2]](https://ide.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/Pennycook%20et%20al%20-%20Shifting%20attention%20to%20accuracy.pdf). NFC could plausibly predict:
- belief (via heuristic reliance),
- sharing (via urgency + social norms),
- correction resistance (via freezing),
but these should be tested separately.

4) **Facet-level precision.**  
Because NFCS facets differ (and decisiveness has measurement complications) [[43]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17259586/), [[44]](https://biblio.ugent.be/publication/1255265/file/6753166.pdf), inconsistent results may partly reflect mixing distinct processes: intolerance of ambiguity vs decisiveness vs closed-mindedness. More work should use revised scales and report facet effects.

5) **When high NFC protects against misinformation.**  
Marchlewska et al. show high NFCC can reduce conspiracy endorsement when official explanations provide closure [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf). Identifying when NFC acts as a protective factor (by freezing on accurate accounts) is as important as identifying when it is a risk factor.

---

### Bottom line: NFC’s role in misinformation acceptance
Need for closure primarily shapes misinformation acceptance by **changing how people seek, evaluate, and stop processing information**. When uncertainty is high and misinformation offers an accessible, coherent explanation, high NFC tends to increase the probability of **seizing** on it; after adoption, high NFC can increase persistence via **freezing**, making corrections less effective. Empirically, NFC is usually a **small-to-modest** predictor relative to factors like **institutional trust** [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/social-psychology/articles/10.3389/frsps.2024.1447313/full) and often less robust than **analytic thinking** in headline discernment research [[1]](https://moodle2.units.it/pluginfile.php/585423/mod_resource/content/0/Pennycook%20%20Rand%20%282019%29.pdf), [[63]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409329121). Its effects are **highly conditional** on context—especially uncertainty, availability of clear official explanations, and salience/accessibility of conspiratorial narratives [[52]](https://kar.kent.ac.uk/61588/1/Marchlewska%20et%20al.%20Addicted%20to%20Answers%20FINAL.pdf).
